title: 2.9 — Naming collisions and an introduction to namespaces

list: Two (or more) identically named functions (or global variables) are introduced into separate files belonging to the same program. This will result in a linker error, as shown above.Two (or more) identically named functions (or global variables) are introduced into the same file. This will result in a compiler error.
Two (or more) identically named functions (or global variables) are introduced into the same file. This will result in a compiler error.

content.text: Key insightA name declared within a scope region (such as a namespace) is distinct from any identical name declared in another scope.
A name declared within a scope region (such as a namespace) is distinct from any identical name declared in another scope.
Key insightA namespace may only contain declarations and definitions. Executable statements are only allowed as part of a definition (e.g. of a function).
A namespace may only contain declarations and definitions. Executable statements are only allowed as part of a definition (e.g. of a function).

list: Identifiers declared inside the global scope are in scope from the point of declaration to the end of the file.Although variables can be defined in the global namespace, this should generally be avoided (we discuss why in lesson7.8 -- Why (non-const) global variables are evil).
Although variables can be defined in the global namespace, this should generally be avoided (we discuss why in lesson7.8 -- Why (non-const) global variables are evil).

content.text: Key insightWhen you use an identifier that is defined inside a non-global namespace (e.g. thestdnamespace), you need to tell the compiler that the identifier lives inside the namespace.”
When you use an identifier that is defined inside a non-global namespace (e.g. thestdnamespace), you need to tell the compiler that the identifier lives inside the namespace.”
Best practiceUse explicit namespace prefixes to access identifiers defined in a namespace.
Use explicit namespace prefixes to access identifiers defined in a namespace.
WarningAvoid using-directives (such asusing namespace std;) at the top of your program or in header files. They violate the reason why namespaces were added in the first place.
Avoid using-directives (such asusing namespace std;) at the top of your program or in header files. They violate the reason why namespaces were added in the first place.
Related contentWe talk more about using-declarations and using-directives (and how to use them responsibly) in lesson7.13 -- Using declarations and using directives.
We talk more about using-declarations and using-directives (and how to use them responsibly) in lesson7.13 -- Using declarations and using directives.

The code that exists inside a nested scope region is conventionally indented one level, both for readability and to help indicate that it exists inside a separate scope region.The#includeand function definitions forfoo()andmain()exist in the global scope region, so they are not indented. The statements inside each function exist inside the nested scope region of the function, so they are indented one level.Next lesson2.10Introduction to the preprocessorBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson2.8Programs with multiple code files
